publicationstmt.publisher: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2023-06-25 00:05:40 UTC.
profiledesc.langusage: Description is written in: Undetermined, Code for undetermined script script.

change.item: This resource was modified by the ArchivesSpace Preprocessor developed by the Harvard Library (https://github.com/harvard-library/archivesspace-preprocessor)

repository.corpname: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

did.physdesc: Scrapbooks, correspondence, broadsides, pamphlets, historical sketches, clippings, addresses, manuscripts, printed material.
did.abstract: Annotated scrapbooks include correspondence, broadsides, pamphlets, historical sketches, addresses, clippings, and editorials relating to his positions, and to Republican and Progressive Party politics, conditions in Cuba and Puerto Rico, education and welfare of New York Indians, and genealogical data on Andrews and Clark families. Also, correspondence, addresses, manuscript essays, school newspapers, and other printed items.

defitem.label: Contact Information:
defitem.item: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections 2B Carl A. Kroch Library Cornell University Ithaca, NY 14853 (607) 255-3530 Fax: (607) 255-9524 rareref@cornell.edu http://rmc.library.cornell.edu

bioghist.head: BIOGRAPHICAL NOTE
bioghist.p: Charles T. Andrews was a teacher in Watkins Glen, Jacksonville, Reynoldsville, and North Hector, New York, and Owatonna, Minnesota, also an employee of newspapers in Watkins Glen and South Bend, Illinois; he later served as a school commissioner of Schuyler County; Seneca Falls school superintendent; agent of New York Secretary of State supervising absentee voting of soldiers in Cuba and Puerto Rico, 1899; Inspector of State Schools, 1900-1904.

scopecontent.head: COLLECTION DESCRIPTION
scopecontent.p: Annotated scrapbooks include correspondence, broadsides, pamphlets, historical sketches, addresses, clippings, and editorials relating to his positions, and to Republican and Progressive Party politics, conditions in Cuba and Puerto Rico, education and welfare of New York Indians, and genealogical data on Andrews and Clark families. Also, correspondence, addresses, manuscript essays, school newspapers, and other printed items.

prefercite.head: Cite As:
prefercite.p: Charles Thomas Andrews papers, #2180. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.

controlaccess.subject: Journalism.
Indians of North America -- New York (State)
Education -- New York (State)
Families -- New York (State)

did.unittitle: Letter to Mary Andrews from Uncle William's wife Elmina (Andrews), son Horace and daughter Maria
Lulu Andrews' school literary paper, "The Evening Star"
Poems written by Charles Andews for Mary
Charles Andrews: school essays as pupil in district school
Essay on "The Soul" by C. Andrews
"A Glance at Contemporaneous History," by C. Andrews
Educational lecture at Owatonna, Minn. by C. Andrews
Partial index of the copies of the Watkins, NY Express bound in the cover "Charles T. Andrews Papers - Watkins Express"
Arthur Andrews' letters printed in the Seneca County Courier (newsclippings)
Scrapbook pages of "Letters from the West"
"True Lincoln Story" while on magazine, South Indiana
"Golden Wedding" of Charles and Mary Andrews
C. Andrews: School Commissioners Record Book
Mynderse Commencement Address by C. Andrews
Mynderse Commencement Address by C. Andrews
Mss. of school addresses - Plattsburg Normal
"The Seneca County Courier," "Centennial of the Schools"
"Hector Township in the Military Tract - Then and Now" by C. Andrews
"Hector - Then and Now" by C. Andrews
"New Harmony - The Seed Bed of Modern Progress" by C. Andrews
Editorials possible for Eagle May by C. Andrews
Volumes (all by Charles T. Andrews unless otherwise noted)
Churches and Pastors of Seneca County, N.Y. compiled and published by Courier Printing Company, Seneca Falls, NY
The Two Orphans of Marrakech and Other Dramatic Spots, by David Dunham, inscribed by the author to Prof. and Mrs. Andrews
